results.guideline_title: The value of an expression and its persistent side effects shall be the same under all permitted evaluation orders

explanation.summary: The code violates Rule 13.2 because the value of an expression and its persistent side effects shall be the same under all permitted evaluation orders. Side effects in complex expressions make behavior harder to predict and verify.
explanation.rule_basis: The Standard gives considerable flexibility to compilers when evaluating expressions. Most operators can have their operands evaluated in any order. The main exceptions are: • Logical AND && in which the second operand is evaluated only if the first operand evaluates to non-zero; • Logical OR || in which the second operand is evaluated only if the first operand evaluates to zero; • The conditional operator ?: in which the first operand is always evaluated and then either the second or third operand is evaluated; • The , operator in which the first operand is evaluated and then the second operand is evaluated. Note: The presence of parentheses may alter the order in which operators are applied. However, this does not affect the order of evaluation of the lowest-level operands, which may be evaluated in any order. Many of the common instances of the unpredictable behavior associated with expression evaluation can be avoided by following the advice given by Rule 13.3 and Rule 13.4.

risk_analysis.why: The violation of Rule 13.2 can lead to unpredictable behavior and potential runtime errors.
potential_failures: Runtime errors due to unpredictable behavior

deviation_advice.recommended_decision: Implement the fix to comply with the rule.
deviation_advice.required_justification: Non-compliance with MISRA guidelines can lead to unpredictable behavior and potential runtime errors.
deviation_advice.review_notes: Ensure that all fixes are thoroughly tested to avoid unintended consequences.

explanation.summary: The value of 'output' (uint16_t) is assigned to 'scaled' (uint8_t). This implicit narrowing conversion may silently truncate the value, causing data loss and incorrect program behavior.
explanation.rule_basis: The rationale is described in the introduction on composite operators and expressions (see Section 8.1 0.3). Casting to a wider type is not permitted as the result may vary between implementations. Consider the following: ( uint32_t ) ( u16a + u16b ); On a 16-bit machine the addition will be performed in 16 bits with the result wrapping modulo- 2 before it is cast to 32 bits. However, on a 32-bit machine the addition will take place in 32 bits and would preserve high-order bits that would have been lost on a 16-bit machine. Casting to a narrower type with the same essential type of the result always leads to the same loss of information.
explanation.code_evidence: The code snippet provided shows an implicit narrowing conversion from uint16_t to uint8_t.

fix_suggestions.title: Explicit cast
fix_suggestions.why: To avoid data loss and incorrect program behavior due to implicit narrowing conversion.
fix_suggestions.compliance_notes: This fix ensures that the value is correctly cast to the appropriate type.

fix_suggestions.title: Use a wider data type
fix_suggestions.why: To prevent data loss and incorrect program behavior due to implicit narrowing conversion.
fix_suggestions.compliance_notes: This fix ensures that the value is temporarily stored in a wider data type before being cast to the appropriate type.

fix_suggestions.title: Use a function to handle the conversion
fix_suggestions.why: To prevent data loss and incorrect program behavior due to implicit narrowing conversion.
fix_suggestions.compliance_notes: This fix ensures that the conversion is handled in a separate function, making the code easier to maintain and understand.
